blocks.heading: Formal Respect
blocks.text: In German medical settings, maintaining a professional distance is mandatory to show respect.
bullets: Always address the doctor as 'Herr Doktor [Last Name]' or 'Frau Doktor [Last Name]'.
Address clinic staff as 'Herr [Last Name]' or 'Frau [Last Name]'.
Never use first names with medical professionals.
Always use the formal 'Sie' (you).

blocks.heading: The Hausarzt (GP) System
blocks.text: The General Practitioner (GP) acts as the primary gateway to the German healthcare system.
bullets: Do not go to a specialist first; you must see a Hausarzt (GP) for a referral (Überweisung).
Register with a Hausarzt as soon as you arrive in Germany.
Use the 'Doctolib' app to find doctors and book appointments online.

blocks.heading: Medical Notes (Attest)
blocks.text: Universities and employers require formal proof of illness if you miss mandatory sessions.
bullets: If you miss a university exam, ask specifically for a 'Prüfungsunfähigkeitsbescheinigung'.
A standard work note (Krankenschein) may be rejected by the university for exams.
Always get your Attest on the same day you are sick; doctors rarely backdate them.

blocks.heading: Apotheke vs. Drogerie
blocks.text: Germany maintains a strict split between medical pharmacies and general drugstores.
bullets: Go to an 'Apotheke' (Red 'A' sign) for prescription and over-the-counter medicine.
Go to a 'Drogerie' (DM, Rossmann) for hygiene products, vitamins, and toiletries.
Check the pharmacy window for the 'Notdienst' schedule to find a 24/7 pharmacy on Sundays.

blocks.heading: Emergency Numbers
blocks.text: Knowing which number to call can save lives and ensure you get the right level of care.
bullets: Call 112: Only for life-threatening emergencies (accidents, heart attacks).
Call 116 117: For urgent medical issues after-hours or on weekends that are not life-threatening.

blocks.heading: Understanding Prescriptions
blocks.text: The color of the prescription paper determines how much you will pay at the pharmacy.
bullets: Pink/Red: Public insurance covers most costs (you pay a €5–€10 co-pay).
Blue/Green: You must pay the full price for the medication.

blocks.heading: Halal & Dietary Restrictions
blocks.text: Many German medications use animal-based additives that may not be Halal or Vegan.
bullets: Many capsules contain pork-based gelatin.
Ask: 'Ist dieses Medikament ohne Gelatine?' (Is this medicine without gelatin?).
Ask: 'Gibt es eine vegane Alternative?' (Is there a vegan alternative?).
Many medicines are available as liquid drops (Tropfen) instead of capsules.
